tool.name: recetox-xMSannotator: Compute Chemscore
tool.description: compute chemical scores for annotation confidence assessment

configfiles.configfile:
# Load input tables annotation <- load_table("$annotation", "$annotation.ext") global_cor <- load_table("$global_cor", "$global_cor.ext") adduct_weights <- load_table("$adduct_weights", "$adduct_weights.ext")
# Parse filter_by parameter filter_by <- strsplit("$filter_by", ",")[[1]] if (length(filter_by) == 1 && filter_by == "") { filter_by <- NA }
# Compute chemscores using parallel processing # The pmap_dfr pattern iterates over each row and computes scores annotation <- purrr::pmap_dfr( annotation, function(.x, .y, .z, ...) { recetox.xmsannotator::get_chemscore( annotation = annotation, adduct_weights = adduct_weights, corthresh = $corthresh, global_cor = global_cor, max_diff_rt = $max_diff_rt, filter.by = filter_by, outlocorig = tempdir() ) } )
# Save output save_table(annotation, "$output_file", "$output_file.ext")

param.name: annotation
param.label: Reformatted annotation table
param.help: Reformatted annotation table from Reformat Annotation tool. Ready for chemscore computation.

param.name: global_cor
param.label: Global correlation matrix
param.help: Global correlation data from Reformat Annotation tool. Provides context for correlation-based scoring.

param.name: adduct_weights
param.label: Adduct weights table
param.help: Table of adduct weights for scoring different adduct types.

param.name: corthresh
param.label: Correlation threshold
param.help: Minimum correlation required for peaks to be considered as supporting the same chemical identity. Peaks above this threshold contribute to the chemscore calculation.

param.name: max_diff_rt
param.label: Maximum RT difference [s]
param.help: Maximum retention time difference allowed between peaks that are considered as potential adducts/isotopes of the same compound.

tool.help:
**Compute Chemscore**
Calculate chemical scores (chemscores) that quantify the confidence of each annotation.
Description ===========
The chemscore is a comprehensive confidence metric that combines multiple lines of evidence to assess how likely an annotation is correct. Higher scores indicate higher confidence in the annotation.
Scoring Components ------------------
The chemscore algorithm considers:
1. **Correlation Evidence** - How well does this peak correlate with other peaks in its module? - Do supporting peaks (potential adducts/isotopes) show high correlation?
2. **Adduct Expectations** - Are the observed adducts consistent with expected patterns? - Weighted by adduct-specific probabilities from the adduct_weights table
3. **Isotope Patterns** - Are isotopes detected and do they match theoretical patterns? - More isotopes = higher confidence
4. **RT Consistency** - Do all supporting peaks elute within the expected RT window? - Co-elution supports common origin
5. **Module Membership** - Are supporting ions in the same correlation module? - Module coherence increases confidence
Parameters ----------
**Correlation Threshold (corthresh)** Minimum correlation (0-1) for peaks to be considered as supporting the same compound. - Lower (0.6-0.7): More inclusive, may include false positives - Higher (0.8-0.9): Stricter, may miss true but weak correlations - Default: 0.7
**Maximum RT Difference** Maximum allowed RT difference (seconds) between supporting peaks. Should match your chromatographic peak width characteristics.
**Filter By Adducts** Which adduct types to consider for scoring. Selecting specific adducts can: - Focus scoring on relevant ionization modes - Reduce noise from rare/unexpected adducts
**Parallel Processing** This tool uses `purrr::pmap_dfr` for parallel row-wise processing. Galaxy job slots (${GALAXY_SLOTS}) are automatically utilized.
Inputs ------
**Reformatted Annotation** Output from Reformat Annotation tool.
**Global Correlation** Global correlation data from Reformat Annotation tool.
**Adduct Weights** Adduct weight table for scoring different adduct types.
Outputs -------
**Annotation with Chemscores** The annotation table with additional score columns: - `score`: Overall chemscore value - Supporting evidence breakdown (may vary by version)
Score Interpretation --------------------
Chemscores range from 0 to 100,000 (1e5) in the default implementation: - High scores (>10,000): Strong evidence, high confidence - Medium scores (1,000-10,000): Moderate evidence - Low scores (<1,000): Weak evidence, consider manual review
Note: Exact score ranges depend on dataset characteristics and parameters.
Downstream Usage ----------------
Chemscores are used by: - **Pathway Matching**: Higher-scoring annotations may get pathway boosts - **Confidence Levels**: Score thresholds determine confidence level assignment - **Redundancy Filtering**: Low-scoring redundant annotations can be removed
Performance Notes -----------------
For large datasets, chemscore computation can be time-consuming due to: - Row-wise iteration over all annotations - Correlation lookups for each annotation - Isotope/adduct pattern matching
Recommendations: - Use appropriate Galaxy job resources (memory, CPU) - Filter to relevant adducts when possible - Consider correlation threshold trade-offs
References ----------
Novotný, J., et al. (2024). recetox-xMSannotator: Advanced metabolite annotation tool. Analytical Chemistry. DOI: 10.1021/acs.analchem.6b01214
Sumner, L. W., et al. (2007). Proposed minimum reporting standards for chemical analysis. Metabolomics, 3(3), 211-221. DOI: 10.1007/s11306-007-0082-2
